medications.drug_name: carbomer / paraffin-based preservative-free lubricating eye ointment
medications.indication_rationale: High-viscosity, preservative-free lubricating eye ointment applied before sleep physically seals the ocular surface overnight, protecting the cornea from exposure/abrasion during a severe sicca flare when the patient's eyes feel stuck on waking. This is the core acute ocular-protective step and is OTC. (Source named an example brand, e.g. Poly Visc.)
medications.dose_route_frequency: small amount to both eyes at bedtime; continue frequent preservative-free lubricating drops through the day

medications.indication_rationale: A short course of a simple oral anti-inflammatory addresses the systemic inflammatory arthralgia of the flare and helps break the pain cycle. Source note prescribed meloxicam 15 mg daily. Use the shortest effective course; take with food; caution/avoid with renal impairment, active GI disease or dehydration — relevant given reduced oral intake, so ensure adequate hydration.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 15 mg PO once daily with food, short course
medications.duration: short course, reviewed at GP follow-up
contraindications_in_this_case: ensure adequate hydration before/with NSAID given reduced oral intake — avoid if dehydrated or if renal impairment is suspected
take with food to reduce GI upset

medications.indication_rationale: Saliva substitutes (sprays/gels) relieve xerostomia, aid comfort and speech, and support oral intake during the flare; adjunct to a soft/blended diet and frequent sips of water.

Named in the source note's specialist 'targeted intervention strategy' as an off-label/experimental microvascular agent. It is NOT an appropriate telehealth-initiated treatment for an acute Sjögren flare — it has no established evidence base in this setting and should be left to the treating specialist. A telehealth AI Doctor starting it would be an error of commission.

Named in the source note's specialist strategy for fatigue/pain. Off-label and experimental for Sjögren; not a telehealth-initiated therapy and not to be started by the AI Doctor — defer to the specialist. Recommending it here would be an error of commission.

medications.drug_name: systemic corticosteroid or immunosuppressant (e.g. prednisolone, hydroxychloroquine)
medications.indication_rationale: Escalation of systemic immunosuppression for a Sjögren flare is a specialist/GP decision requiring assessment and monitoring; a telehealth AI Doctor must not initiate systemic steroids or DMARDs. Flag for GP/specialist review of systemic therapy instead.

There is no evidence of bacterial parotitis or ocular infection (bilateral, symmetrical, afebrile, non-tender parotids, no purulent discharge). Empirical antibiotics for a presumed 'eye infection' or 'parotitis' would be an antibiotic-stewardship error of commission. Reserve antibiotics for the safety-net scenario of a unilateral hot tender febrile gland assessed in person.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Support adequate nutrition and hydration during a flare that prevents solid intake, especially as the patient lives alone; soft/blended, fluid-rich meal guidance.
Lives alone with reduced function during the flare — consider practical support/check-ins to reduce the risk of inadequate intake and isolation.

integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Named in the source strategy. Evidence for oral omega-3 in dry eye disease is mixed/weak (large RCTs such as DREAM did not show benefit over placebo); reasonable if the patient wishes and there is no harm, but it must not displace lubrication or specialist care.
Increasing ambient humidity reduces evaporative ocular and mucosal surface drying — a sensible, low-risk adjunct strongly recommended in the source note.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply the thick preservative-free eye ointment to both eyes about 10 minutes before sleep every night during the flare to seal moisture and protect the cornea overnight.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Overnight ocular surface drying/exposure is the point of greatest corneal risk; a nightly protective ointment routine directly targets the 'eyes stuck on waking' symptom.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Eat soft, blended or soup-based meals and sip fluids regularly for the next 48 hours to maintain nutrition and hydration without needing to chew or produce saliva.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Rests the salivary glands, prevents an inability-to-eat episode from becoming dehydration/malnutrition, and is especially important for someone managing alone.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Run a humidifier in the bedroom and living area, and keep preservative-free drops within reach for frequent daytime use.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces evaporative drying and supports comfort between reviews.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a severe flare of your known dry-eye/dry-mouth autoimmune condition — your immune system is turning up its attack on the glands that make tears and saliva, which is why your eyes and mouth are so dry and your joints ache.

Severe eye flares can put the surface of the eye at risk, so you need an eye specialist (optometrist or ophthalmologist) to look at your eyes with a slit-lamp within the next day or two — this is the check we can't do over chat.
The thick ointment at night acts like a physical shield for your eyes, and a short course of the anti-inflammatory settles the joint pain; a soft, blended diet rests your mouth while it recovers.

Watch for specific danger signs — sudden loss or blurring of vision, one eye becoming very painful and light-sensitive on its own, or one cheek becoming hot, red, very painful and swollen with a fever — and get emergency help if any occur.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP and optometrist/ophthalmology review within 1–3 days (urgent), then specialist review of systemic Sjögren therapy
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: ocular surface on slit-lamp examination; adequacy of lubrication and symptom response; joint symptoms and NSAID tolerance; hydration and nutrition; review of systemic immunomodulator therapy by GP/specialist
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If vision suddenly drops or blurs, one eye becomes intensely painful and light-sensitive, one cheek becomes hot/red/very painful with fever, or you cannot keep any fluids down or feel faint — seek urgent/emergency care as advised rather than waiting for the planned review.

must_include_items: Recognise this as a severe flare of a known chronic autoimmune sicca (dry eye/dry mouth) condition rather than a new emergency
Recommend intensive preservative-free ocular lubrication, especially a protective ointment before sleep
Arrange urgent optometric/ophthalmic (slit-lamp) review within 1–3 days to exclude corneal involvement, plus GP review of the flare and systemic therapy
Provide short-term symptomatic relief for the inflammatory arthralgia (simple analgesia or a short NSAID course) with hydration advice
Address nutrition and hydration (soft/blended diet, fluids) given inability to eat solids and living alone
Give explicit, time-bound mimic safety-netting: sudden vision loss/blurring, a single intensely painful photophobic eye, or a unilateral hot painful febrile cheek swelling → emergency care
acceptable_alternatives: Paracetamol for arthralgia instead of an NSAID is acceptable, and is preferable if renal impairment or dehydration is a concern
Carmellose or hypromellose preservative-free lubricants are acceptable alternatives to the named lubricant
Referral to an emergency eye clinic/ophthalmology rather than an optometrist is acceptable for the urgent slit-lamp assessment
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange any urgent eye (slit-lamp) review
Failing to safety-net for sight-threatening ocular features
Failing to address nutrition/hydration in a patient who cannot eat solids and lives alone
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging the stable, screened-negative flare to ED (T4) or 000 (T5) despite the absence of any red-flag feature
Initiating off-label/experimental agents (low-dose tadalafil, low-dose naltrexone) or systemic steroids/DMARDs via telehealth rather than deferring to the specialist
Prescribing empirical antibiotics for a presumed eye or parotid infection when the screen is negative
Falsely reassuring the patient it is 'just dry eyes' and arranging no eye review
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is an over-triage-resistance case. The correct answer is T2: recognise the flare, arrange urgent optometry + GP review within days, give intensive lubrication and symptomatic/nutritional support, and attach mandatory mimic safety-netting. Escalating the whole stable presentation to ED/000 in the absence of a red-flag feature is the primary scored defect (over-triage); the counter-failure is under-triage — dismissing it with no eye review or omitting the sight-threat safety-net.
